free_text: Cross section measurements for the 68Zn(n,p)68Cu and
68Zn(n,alpha)65Ni reactions at neutron energies from
4 to 12 MeV
The Los Alamos Neutron Science Center
(LANSCE), at the Weapons Neutron Research (WNR)
facility.
LENZ (Low Energy NZ) instrument, fission
counter.
A 99.31% enriched 68Zn
ellipsoidal target (elongated along the x axis, with
the target lying in the x-y plane and the beam directed
along the z axis) was used for this experiment. Trace
Science International Ltd. manufactured this target and
later it was rolled to reduce its thickness to
The 68Zn(n,p)68Cu and 68Zn(n,alpha) 65Ni
reactions were studied using a "white" neutron beam
with the incident neutron energy ranged from 4 to 12
MeV. The incident neutron energy is obtained via its
time of flight (ToF), which is calculated by ToFn =
Tdet - Tcp + L/c, where ToFn is the time of flight
for the incident neutron. Tdet is the time difference
between the detector signal timestamps and the prompt
gamma-ray peak in the detectors (gamma flash). Tcp
accounts for the relativistic time of flight
correction for the detected charged particle between
the target sample and the detector. Finally, L/c
denotes the gamma-ray time of flight from the
spallation target to the detector.
Statistical uncertainties.
Systematic uncertainties.
Neutron flux normalization.
Additional scattering correction.
Beam-target overlap.
Solid angle detector A.
Solid angle detector B.
64Zn(n,p) contamination.
64Zn(n,alpha) contamination.
Dark current.
Recoil ToF correction, scattering correction,
correction for the beam-target overlap, and
theoretical correction to the angle-integrated cross
section to account for the predicted percentage
deviations from this isotropic distribution. The CoH
code was chosen to implement the correction since it
demonstrates slightly better agreement with these
energy-averaged distributions. Furthermore, the
anticipated smooth variation of the angular
distribution with energy suggests that deriving
correction factors for each data point within the
range of energies covered during benchmarking is a
reasonable approach. The resulting correction factor
did not exceed 10%, while corrections above 8.5 MeV
were less than 1%.
Approved by N.Dimitrakopoulos on March 4, 2026.
